content: Hey @Martin Rodriguez Reboredo you might know already but looks like your last email hit the list three times - were those meant to be separate patches?
Ahh, yes, happened that I've confused the usage of -n with git-send-email instead of --dry-run, only the last email should be considered, sorry for the noise.

I find it kind of surprising that git-send-email is still the pinnacle of mailing list technology. I've been in the habit of sending things to myself as a dry run, a couple days ago I didn't realize that it CCs every email in any commit message by default...

I've sent the email using the following command under the linux tree.

git send-email --to="rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org" \
    --cc-cmd="./scripts/get_maintainer.pl --no-rolestats v3-0001-scripts*.patch" \
    v3-0001-scripts-generate_rust_analyzer-provide-cfg-s-for-.patch

Indeed, it's very powerful, you can also test it using the --dry-run flag with it. And certainly, the reason of this thread is because -n is a common shortcut of --dry-run in git, take for instance git-clean, although if you pass it to git-send-email it seems that it'll be ignored and things won't get run dry. Perhaps I should do a PR for git.
It doesn't even look like -n does anything for that command, that's a bit concerning even

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

I've sent the email using the following command under the linux tree.

git send-email --to="rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org" \
    --cc-cmd="./scripts/get_maintainer.pl --no-rolestats v3-0001-scripts*.patch" \
    v3-0001-scripts-generate_rust_analyzer-provide-cfg-s-for-.patch

Indeed, it's very powerful, you can also test it using the --dry-run flag with it. And certainly, the reason of this thread is because -n is a common shortcut of --dry-run in git, take for instance git-clean, although if you pass it to git-send-email it seems that it'll be ignored and things won't get run dry. Perhaps I should do a PR for git.

You can put config into .git/config so that you don't have to repeat. E.g. I use this config:

[sendemail]
tocmd = scripts/get_maintainer.pl --norolestats --nol
cccmd = scripts/get_maintainer.pl --norolestats --nom --nor

Gary Guo said:

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

I've sent the email using the following command under the linux tree.

git send-email --to="rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org" \
    --cc-cmd="./scripts/get_maintainer.pl --no-rolestats v3-0001-scripts*.patch" \
    v3-0001-scripts-generate_rust_analyzer-provide-cfg-s-for-.patch

Indeed, it's very powerful, you can also test it using the --dry-run flag with it. And certainly, the reason of this thread is because -n is a common shortcut of --dry-run in git, take for instance git-clean, although if you pass it to git-send-email it seems that it'll be ignored and things won't get run dry. Perhaps I should do a PR for git.

You can put config into .git/config so that you don't have to repeat. E.g. I use this config:

[sendemail]
tocmd = scripts/get_maintainer.pl --norolestats --nol
cccmd = scripts/get_maintainer.pl --norolestats --nom --nor

This one looks useful, thanks for the tip!
On that note I can recommend git-publish https://github.com/stefanha/git-publish . It adds a bit of features to git-send-email. I find the patch versioning feature useful.
